meta.lecture: Control of Microorganisms — Physical, Chemical & Mechanical (06/22/2025)
meta.source: Professor's PowerPoint (63 slides). No lecture recording exists for this deck.
meta.note: All cards are drawn from the slide deck. Import into Anki/Quizlet as Q/A pairs.

cards.q: What are the only two things you can do to control microorganisms?
cards.a: Kill them, or inhibit their growth.

cards.q: What does 'microbial death' mean?
cards.a: The cell takes irreversible damage and can no longer reproduce, even under ideal conditions.

cards.q: How does a microbial population die off once the killing threshold is reached?
cards.a: Logarithmically — a fixed percentage dies each interval, so the last few cells take the longest.

cards.q: Define sterilization.
cards.a: Destruction or removal of ALL microorganisms and viruses in or on an object.

cards.q: Define disinfection.
cards.a: Destruction of MOST microbes and viruses on a nonliving surface. It leaves endospores alive.

cards.q: Define antisepsis.
cards.a: Reducing the number of microbes on LIVING tissue (e.g., iodine or alcohol on skin).

cards.q: Define degerming.
cards.a: The mechanical removal of microbes from a surface — e.g., hand washing or an alcohol swab.

cards.q: Define sanitization.
cards.a: Reducing microbes to safe public-health levels (e.g., scalding dishwater in restaurants).

cards.q: Define pasteurization.
cards.a: Using heat to destroy pathogens and reduce spoilage organisms in foods/beverages.

cards.q: What do the suffixes -cide and -static mean?
cards.a: -cide/-cidal = kills (bactericide); -stasis/-static = inhibits growth without killing (bacteriostatic).

cards.q: True or false: something can be 'almost sterile.'
cards.a: False. 'Sterile' is absolute — zero viable microbes. Disinfection/sanitization only reduce the load.

cards.q: Put the germicidal spectrum in order, weakest to strongest.
cards.a: Cleaner → Sanitizer → Disinfectant → Virucide → Sterilant.

cards.q: Which level on the germicidal spectrum is the only one that eliminates spores?
cards.a: The sterilant — it eliminates ALL fungi, bacteria, viruses, AND spores.

cards.q: What does a cleaner do vs. a sanitizer?
cards.a: A cleaner aids in soil (dirt) removal; a sanitizer reduces the NUMBER of bacteria to safe levels.

cards.q: Name the three categories of microbial control.
cards.a: Physical (a physical force), Chemical (chemicals), and Mechanical (physical removal of microbes).

cards.q: What are the 'age-old methods,' and what's their limitation?
cards.a: Osmotic pressure (salting/sugaring) and refrigeration — they suppress growth but do NOT kill.

cards.q: Figure 11.1 — which outcome does mechanical removal (filtration) reach?
cards.a: Decontamination — the mechanical removal of most microbes (it removes, it doesn't kill).

cards.q: Disinfection destroys vegetative pathogens but NOT what?
cards.a: Bacterial endospores. That's why disinfection isn't sterilization.

cards.q: Name the main ways antimicrobial agents damage microbes.
cards.a: Alter cell walls/membranes, and damage proteins and nucleic acids (DNA/RNA).

cards.q: What happens when an agent disrupts the cell wall?
cards.a: The cell can't resist osmotic pressure and bursts.

cards.q: What happens when an agent damages the cytoplasmic membrane?
cards.a: Cellular contents leak out.

cards.q: Why does damaging a viral envelope disable the virus?
cards.a: The envelope is needed for the virus to attach to its target cell — no attachment, no infection.

cards.q: What does it mean to 'denature' a protein, and what causes it?
cards.a: Unfolding its 3-D shape so it stops working; caused by extreme heat or certain chemicals.

cards.q: List the qualities of an 'ideal' antimicrobial agent.
cards.a: Inexpensive, fast-acting, stable in storage, and controls all microbes while staying harmless to humans, animals, and objects.

cards.q: Why does 'site to be treated' affect method choice?
cards.a: Harsh chemicals and extreme heat can't be used on people, animals, or fragile objects — the method must match the site.

cards.q: Name factors (besides site) that change how well a method works.
cards.a: Number and nature of microbes, temperature/pH, agent concentration, mechanism of action, and organic matter/solvents/inhibitors.

cards.q: Why does organic matter (blood, feces, dirt) reduce a disinfectant's effect?
cards.a: It shields microbes and can neutralize the disinfectant.

cards.q: What does a HIGH-level germicide kill?
cards.a: All pathogens, including endospores (essentially sterilizing).

cards.q: What does an INTERMEDIATE-level germicide kill?
cards.a: Fungal spores, protozoan cysts, viruses, and pathogenic bacteria — but NOT endospores.

cards.q: What does a LOW-level germicide kill?
cards.a: Vegetative bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and some viruses.

cards.q: What is the phenol coefficient?
cards.a: An agent's killing power expressed as a ratio to phenol. >1.0 = more effective than phenol. It's an older, largely replaced method.

cards.q: General rule: high vs. low temperatures?
cards.a: Higher temperatures are microbicidal (kill); lower temperatures are microbistatic (inhibit).

cards.q: What is the autoclave, and what are its standard settings?
cards.a: Steam under pressure — the preferred sterilization method. 15 psi raises steam to 121°C (~15 min).

cards.q: What is the ONLY thing the autoclave doesn't kill?
cards.a: Prions. It kills all other microorganisms.

cards.q: Why does pressurizing steam matter (Figure 9.5)?
cards.a: Pressure raises the steam's temperature — plain boiling water tops out at 100°C, but pressurized steam reaches 121°C.

cards.q: Define Thermal Death Time (TDT).
cards.a: The shortest time required to kill all test microbes at a specified temperature.

cards.q: Define Thermal Death Point (TDP).
cards.a: The lowest temperature that kills all microbes in a sample in 10 minutes.

cards.q: Flash vs. batch pasteurization?
cards.a: Flash: 71.6°C for 15 seconds. Batch: 63–66°C for 30 minutes.

cards.q: Does pasteurization sterilize? What does it fail to kill?
cards.a: No — it's disinfection. It does NOT kill endospores or thermoduric (heat-tolerant) microbes.

cards.q: Boiling water — sterilization or disinfection?
cards.a: Disinfection only (~30 min). It kills vegetative cells but not endospores.

cards.q: Hot-air (dry heat) oven — settings and use?
cards.a: 150–180°C for 2–4 hours; for heat-resistant items that don't sterilize well with moist heat. Also effective against endotoxin.

cards.q: Which control method is uniquely effective against endotoxin?
cards.a: Prolonged dry heat (hot-air oven) can destroy the gram-negative endotoxin.

cards.q: Why does moist heat beat dry heat?
cards.a: Steam carries more energy and penetrates better, so moist heat kills faster and at lower temperatures.

cards.q: Which three radiations does the deck list for microbial control?
cards.a: Gamma rays, X-rays, and ultraviolet (UV).

cards.q: Ionizing vs. nonionizing radiation — penetration and outcome?
cards.a: Ionizing (gamma/X-ray): deeply penetrating, destroys DNA, sterilizes. Nonionizing (UV): shallow, disinfects.

cards.q: What wavelength does a germicidal UV lamp use?
cards.a: 254 nm (UV overall spans ~100–400 nm).

cards.q: Why does UV only disinfect rather than sterilize?
cards.a: It's not penetrating — it only reaches microbes it directly shines on; shadowed or embedded microbes survive.

cards.q: Typical uses of ionizing radiation?
cards.a: Sterilizing food products and single-use medical products (it penetrates packaging).

cards.q: Typical uses of UV radiation?
cards.a: Disinfecting hospital/operating rooms, schools, food-prep areas, dental offices; treating drinking water.

cards.q: What does refrigeration do to microbes — and not do?
cards.a: It slows metabolism and halts growth of most pathogens, but it does NOT kill them.

cards.q: What is a psychrophile, and why does it matter for the fridge?
cards.a: A cold-loving microbe that can still multiply in refrigerated food — why the fridge only delays spoilage.

cards.q: Slow vs. quick freezing — which is more damaging to cells?
cards.a: Slow freezing is more damaging (larger ice crystals).

cards.q: Desiccation — how does it work and how strong is it?
cards.a: Drying removes water and inhibits growth; it is microbiostatic only (many microbes survive and revive).

cards.q: What is lyophilization used for?
cards.a: Long-term preservation of microbial cultures — freeze-drying that avoids damaging ice crystals.

cards.q: How does osmotic pressure inhibit microbes?
cards.a: High salt/sugar makes the environment hypertonic; cells lose water and desiccate.

cards.q: Do bacteria or fungi tolerate hypertonic (salty/sugary) conditions better?
cards.a: Fungi — which is why mold, not bacteria, grows on jam.

cards.q: What is a tincture?
cards.a: An antimicrobial agent dissolved in alcohol (or a water–alcohol mix).

cards.q: Phenolics — examples and a key advantage?
cards.a: Lysol and triclosan; they stay active even in the presence of organic matter and last a long time (downside: odor).

cards.q: Why is 70% ethanol used to swab skin, and what's its drawback?
cards.a: It denatures proteins and disrupts membranes (intermediate-level); it evaporates rapidly, which shortens contact time.

cards.q: Name common halogen antiseptics/disinfectants.
cards.a: Iodine and iodophors (Betadine), chlorine in drinking water, bleach, chloramines in dressings, bromine in hot tubs.

cards.q: Chlorhexidine — spectrum and limitation?
cards.a: Bactericidal for gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, but inactive against spores; mild, low toxicity, rapid action.

cards.q: Which chemical family is high-level and how does it kill?
cards.a: Oxidizing agents (peroxides, ozone, peracetic acid) — they oxidize microbial enzymes.

cards.q: Why isn't hydrogen peroxide useful on open wounds?
cards.a: Catalase in tissue neutralizes it. (It can still disinfect/sterilize surfaces.)

cards.q: Which oxidizing agent is an effective sporocide for equipment?
cards.a: Peracetic acid.

cards.q: Are soaps antimicrobial?
cards.a: No — soaps are excellent degerming agents (they lift microbes off to be rinsed away) but are not antimicrobial.

cards.q: What are quats, and what level of disinfectant are they?
cards.a: Quaternary ammonium compounds — positively charged surfactants; human-safe; LOW-level disinfectants.

cards.q: How do heavy metals kill, and what level are they?
cards.a: They alter the 3-D shape of proteins; low-level, bacteriostatic/fungistatic.

cards.q: Give three clinical/practical uses of heavy metals.
cards.a: 1% silver nitrate to prevent newborn gonococcal blindness; thimerosal to preserve vaccines; copper to control algae.

cards.q: Glutaraldehyde — what can it do?
cards.a: It disinfects with short exposure and sterilizes with long exposure (an aldehyde that cross-links proteins).

cards.q: What is formalin used for?
cards.a: Embalming and disinfecting rooms/instruments (it's a formaldehyde solution).

cards.q: Name the gaseous (gas) sterilants and their use.
cards.a: Ethylene oxide, propylene oxide, beta-propiolactone — closed-chamber sterilization of heat/moisture-sensitive items.

cards.q: What's the danger of gaseous sterilants like ethylene oxide?
cards.a: They're hazardous — often explosive, extremely poisonous, and potentially carcinogenic.

cards.q: What does overuse of antiseptics and disinfectants promote?
cards.a: The development of resistant microbes — with little evidence that heavy use improves health.

cards.q: What does mechanical control do differently from physical/chemical control?
cards.a: It physically REMOVES microbes from the environment rather than killing them (filtration, airflow).

cards.q: How does filtration work?
cards.a: Fluid or air is strained through a filter whose pores let fluid pass but are too small for microorganisms.

cards.q: What are membrane filters made of?
cards.a: Thin sheets of cellulose acetate, polycarbonate, or plastic with a controlled, standardized pore size.

cards.q: Give practical uses of filtration.
cards.a: Sterilizing heat-sensitive liquids, decontaminating beverages without changing flavor, water purification, and HEPA air filtration.

cards.q: What does a HEPA filter do?
cards.a: Removes airborne microbes (bacteria and viruses) — used in cabin air, operating rooms, and clean rooms.

cards.q: Filter pore size vs. what's trapped — what's the rule?
cards.a: The smaller the pore, the smaller the microbe it catches. A 0.22 µm filter traps most bacteria (the standard for sterilizing liquids); smaller pores are needed for viruses.

cards.q: Which spots do people commonly miss when hand washing (Grimebusters)?
cards.a: Fingertips, under the nails, thumbs, between the fingers, wrists, and the backs of the hands.

cards.q: What is the single hardest microbial form to kill, and which methods reach it?
cards.a: The bacterial endospore — only high-level/sterilizing methods (autoclave, incineration, dry heat, ionizing radiation, ethylene oxide, peracetic acid, long glutaraldehyde).
